title: Router uses private `_extractParams` API (encapsulation break / fragile coupling)
description: `extractParams()` reads `router._extractParams(path)` (line 120) — a method starting with `_`, indicating private internals. The fallback `routerMatchPublic()` re-iterates a hard-coded list `_publicPatterns` (lines 125–128) that must be kept in sync with the route registrations above (lines 65–66). If a developer adds a new dynamic route and forgets to update `_publicPatterns`, params will silently resolve to `{}` and the page receives `params.projectId === undefined`. This is a maintenance landmine for the app entry point.
suggested_fix: Export `extractParams(pattern, path)` as a public router method (or expose `params` on the `ctx` passed to handlers). In `loadPage`, capture `ctx.params` from the resolved route match (the router already computes them in `resolve()`). Delete `_publicPatterns` and the `_extractParams` private access.

title: `navigate()` short-circuits identical path, dropping query string changes and breaking back/forward
description: `if (path === this._currentPath && !opts.replace) return` (line 170) bails on same path with new query. So `router.navigate('/projects?tab=overview')` after `/projects?tab=activity` does nothing — no `pushState`, no `resolve()`, no `popstate`. The URL bar is also not updated, so the user has no way to deep-link or share the new query state. Common in tab/sort/pagination interactions.
suggested_fix: Compare full `path + search` (e.g. store `_currentHref = location.pathname + location.search`). Or remove the early-return entirely — `pushState` is cheap and the page can decide to skip work via guard. At minimum, treat same-path-different-query as a real navigation.

title: Link interceptor only intercepts left-click; middle-click and right-click open in new tab lose SPA nav (acceptable) but no fallback handling
description: Lines 192–219 ignore `e.button !== 0` (any non-left click returns early without `preventDefault`), so middle-click (`button === 1`) and right-click contexts still let the browser handle `e.defaultPrevented` check. The bigger issue: intercepted links are written via `history.pushState` but the link is now no longer a real `<a>` for the browser's "Open in new tab" — middle-click goes to the current `href` (good), but the user has no way to know if a click will be SPA-nav'd or full-reload. The `dataset.external` check at line 209 only handles `data-external="true"` — there is no way to opt out of interception for a specific link.
suggested_fix: Add a way to mark links as non-SPA (e.g. `data-spa="false"` skip rule), and document the `data-external="true"` convention. Consider respecting `e.button === 1` to keep middle-click "open in new tab" working unchanged (it already does, just confirm).

title: Global `popstate` listener registered on module import — not removable, leaks in tests / HMR
description: `window.addEventListener('popstate', () => router.resolve())` is at module top level (line 227). There is no matching `removeEventListener`. Under Vite HMR or repeated test imports, each reload adds another `popstate` listener that captures the *previous* module's `router` — causing duplicated `router.resolve()` calls per back/forward press, with stale references. The comment says "idempotent" but `addEventListener` is not idempotent.
suggested_fix: Register the `popstate` listener in `bootstrap()` (or an explicit `router.start()` method) and store the bound handler so it can be removed in `dispose()`. Guard with a flag (`if (this._popstateBound) return`).

title: CSRF token sent in `X-CSRF-Token` header, but Next.js /api/* with withRoute may expect different header
description: Lines 142–148 attach the raw NextAuth double-submit cookie value as `X-CSRF-Token`. NextAuth.js v5 (used in this project's `lib/auth.ts` per AGENTS.md) actually checks the `next-auth.csrf-token` cookie against the request body `csrfToken` field for POST sign-out, but the project's `withRoute` HOF reads CSRF via a custom mechanism (and the codebase comment in shell.js line 192 admits: 'NextAuth signout endpoint is GET-style; alternative POST below'). When a real POST hits `withRoute` and the header is missing or the cookie is stale, the request will fail with 403. The current client trusts that adding the header is enough — but the comment in shell.js (line 192) explicitly says it's not.
suggested_fix: Verify which CSRF header `withRoute` actually checks (likely a custom header set by the project, not `X-CSRF-Token`). If NextAuth standard, the `csrfToken` body field is also needed for sign-out. Add an integration test that POSTs to a real withRoute endpoint with a stale cookie and asserts failure, then with a fresh cookie and asserts success.

title: Hard `location.href` redirect on 401 breaks SPA back-button history and races with in-flight requests
description: On any 401 (line 161), the client does `location.href = '/login?next=...'` (line 166). This is a full page reload, throwing away: (a) the current SPA state, (b) any in-flight query promises (they'll reject with the 401 response, no retry), (c) the URL's back/forward history (the user can't `back` to the page they were on — they get a 401 loop). The `initSession` case is correctly excluded, but normal in-app requests during token expiry all hit this branch.
suggested_fix: On 401, try a single session refresh (`initSession()`). If still anonymous, use `router.navigate('/login?next=...')` instead of `location.href` so the user can `back`. Add a per-request `retried` flag to prevent infinite refresh loops.

title: `opDialog` accepts `body: string` and renders via `innerHTML` — XSS sink if any caller passes user-controlled text
description: Line 194: `else if (typeof opts.body === 'string') body.innerHTML = opts.body`. The `body` prop is documented as `HTMLElement` in the JSDoc (line 160: `body: HTMLElement`) but the implementation accepts `string` and pipes it to `innerHTML`. Any future caller that passes a server error message, user name, or HTML snippet will inject it raw. This is the exact same pattern as React's deprecated `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` and is a critical XSS vector for Phase 1+ pages that render server error envelopes (the api-client's `unwrap` surfaces `r.data?.error?.message` as an Error — if any page renders that into an opDialog body, it's game over).
suggested_fix: (a) Remove the `string` branch entirely — force callers to pass an `HTMLElement`. (b) If you must accept strings, escape with a TextEncoder + DOMParser. (c) Audit the same `else if (typeof X === 'string') X.innerHTML = X` pattern in `opTabs` (line 305) — same bug, same fix.

title: `opTabs` panel string branch also uses `innerHTML` — same XSS vector as opDialog
description: Line 305: `else if (typeof t.panel === 'string') panel.innerHTML = t.panel`. Identical pattern to opDialog (UI-7). JSDoc says `panel: HTMLElement` (line 265) but the impl accepts strings. Any caller passing a server-rendered HTML string with user content is exposed.
suggested_fix: Same as UI-7: remove string branch or escape. Fix the type signature to match the implementation.

title: `opDropdown` registers `document.addEventListener('click', close)` on every instance — memory leak and never removed
description: Line 258: `document.addEventListener('click', () => { menu.style.display = 'none' })`. The handler closes the *current* dropdown but is never removed. Every `opDropdown` instance adds a permanent document-level listener. If a user logs out, the SPA navigates to /login, the dropdown is removed from DOM, but the document listener still fires on every click anywhere. Over a session this accumulates: open a help dropdown, a settings dropdown, a user dropdown, a sort dropdown, etc. — every click fires all of them. Also the trigger's `e.stopPropagation()` (line 255) doesn't prevent the document listener from firing on the *trigger* click (document listeners are on the bubble path AFTER the trigger).
suggested_fix: Use a single delegated `document.click` listener that walks the DOM to find open `.op-dropdown` and closes them, OR attach the listener to `document` once at bootstrap and let it close any open menu. Track open state via a class on the trigger and a single `currentOpen` reference.

title: `opDialog` keydown listener leaks if multiple dialogs stack; backdrop click closes via stale reference
description: Lines 213–214: each `opDialog` adds `document.addEventListener('keydown', onEsc)` and `backdrop.addEventListener('click', close)`. If two dialogs are open (nested), pressing Esc closes both because both listeners fire. The `backdrop.addEventListener('click', ...)` closes when the *backdrop itself* is clicked — fine — but the escape handler isn't scoped to
. Worse, there's no `focus` trap (a11y) — keyboard users can Tab out of the dialog into the page behind.
suggested_fix: Use a single global `Escape` handler that closes the topmost dialog (stack model). Add focus trap: on open, save `document.activeElement`, focus first focusable inside dialog; on close, restore. Add `aria-labelledby` linking the title to the dialog content. Use a `dialog` element with native `<dialog>` API for free focus trap and Esc handling.

title: `opDropdown` trigger doesn't expose its `aria-expanded` / `aria-haspopup` state — a11y fail
description: The trigger button is created via `opButton({ text: opts.label, variant: 'ghost' })` (line 229) which doesn't set `aria-expanded` to reflect open/closed state, and doesn't set `aria-controls` pointing to the menu id. Screen reader users can't tell if the dropdown is open. The menu also lacks `role="menu"` and items lack `role="menuitem"`. The shell.js user menu (line 173) does set `aria-haspopup` on its *placeholder button* (line 171) but immediately removes that button and replaces it with the dropdown's own button — losing the attribute. So the user menu, the highest-traffic dropdown, is also broken.
suggested_fix: (a) Set `aria-expanded` on trigger, toggle on click, add `aria-controls` pointing to menu id. (b) Add `role="menu"` on menu container, `role="menuitem"` on each button. (c) In shell.js, attach `aria-haspopup` to the dropdown's button (or have opDropdown accept it as a prop).

title: `opTabs` keyboard navigation missing — Arrow Left/Right doesn't move between tabs (a11y)
description: WAI-ARIA tabs pattern requires Arrow Left/Right (or Up/Down for vertical) to move focus and selection between tabs, Home/End for first/last. Line 298 only adds `click` listener. Keyboard-only users are stuck — they can Tab into the tab list but cannot switch tabs. Tabs have `role="tablist"` / `role="tab"` (good) but no `aria-controls` pointing to their panel id, and no keyboard handler.
suggested_fix: Add keydown handler on the tablist: ArrowLeft/Right to move, Home/End to jump. Update `tabindex` so only the active tab is in the tab order (`tabindex="0"` vs `-1`). Add `aria-controls` to each tab pointing to the panel's id, and `aria-labelledby` on each panel pointing back to its tab.

title: `opToastHost` re-renders entire toast list on every signal change — O(n) DOM thrash and listener leak
description: Line 333: `host.innerHTML = ''` followed by full re-append on every signal change. This: (a) discards the existing toast DOM nodes (and any click listeners on them, lines 338 — they're GC'd but the act of rebuilding is wasteful), (b) makes enter/exit animations impossible (no per-toast lifecycle), (c) for a long-lived app this is fine for a few toasts but patterns badly. Also, the `effect` (line 331) is never disposed — if `opToastHost` is called twice, two effects fire on every toast change, double-rendering.
suggested_fix: Make `opToastHost` idempotent: return existing host if already mounted. Use a keyed reconciliation (compare `t.id` to existing children, only add/remove deltas). Track the dispose and call it in a `dispose` returned alongside the host.

title: `opTooltip` registers 4 listeners on `opts.target` with no removal path — leaks if target is removed from DOM
description: Lines 415–418 add `mouseenter`, `mouseleave`, `focus`, `blur` to `opts.target` but expose no `dispose()`. If `opTooltip` is called inside a list (e.g. a work-package table cell with a hover tooltip) and the cell is re-rendered, the old listeners stay on the detached node, plus a new tooltip is created on the new node. Memory grows linearly with table scrolls. Also, `tip` is positioned with `position: fixed` using `rect.top - 30` (line 410) — if `rect.top < 30`, the tooltip renders off-screen above the viewport.
suggested_fix: Return a `dispose` from `opTooltip` that removes all 4 listeners and any active tip. Add viewport-edge clamping: if `rect.top - 30 < 0`, place tooltip at `rect.bottom + 6`.

title: `effect` re-subscribes to all signals on every run — quadratic behavior and memory growth for many effects
description: Lines 105–117: each effect run drops prior subs (line 108), then re-reads signals, calling `s.subscribe(run)` for each. This is O(N*M) for N effects over M signals. For an app with 50 components each `effect`-watching 3 signals, every signal change does 150 set operations. Also, `s.subscribe(run)` adds `run` to the signal's `#subs` Set but `Signal.subscribe` (line 67) doesn't check for duplicates — so if the same effect somehow runs twice without dropping (e.g. concurrent signal changes in a batched update), `run` ends up in the Set multiple times, firing the effect N times for one change.
suggested_fix: (a) De-dupe in `Signal.subscribe`: `if (this.#subs.has(fn)) return fn` then add. (b) Move from per-effect re-subscribe to a true dependency-tracking model (Proxy or explicit `track()` calls), or at minimum memoize the dep set per effect-run. (c) Add a stress test with 1000 effects to catch this.

title: Signal setter iterates subs without copy when NOT in batch — but does copy in batch path; inconsistent + catches concurrent mutation
description: Line 57: `for (const sub of [...this.#subs]) sub()` — copies before iterate. Line 54: `for (const sub of this.#subs) pendingEffects.add(sub)` — does NOT copy. The non-copy case is fine because `pendingEffects.add` is idempotent and doesn't iterate concurrently, but the inconsistency suggests intent isn't clear. More importantly, the batch path adds to `pendingEffects` but the *run* happens after batch commits (line 166) — between the assignment and the run, multiple `effect`s might `subscribe` to a different signal that re-uses the same function, leading to fire-twice.
suggested_fix: Copy in both paths for consistency. Document the batch semantics with a JSDoc on `batch()`. Add a regression test where one effect re-subscribes during batch commit.

title: `_executeEntry` rejects observers with `AbortError` but the new fetch isn't awaited — race condition on rapid invalidation
description: Lines 125–130: when a fetch is aborted (entry replaced by a newer fetch), the OLD observers get `{reject: AbortError}`. But the NEW fetch's promise is `return new Promise((resolve, reject) => { entry.observers.push({ resolve, reject }); exec.then(...).catch(...) })` — the new `entry` reference is shared. If the new fetch's `then` resolves, it iterates `entry.observers` (now containing NEW observers from `_executeEntry` being called again) — wait, actually observers are reset to `[]` after each completion (lines 123, 129, 135). So the new fetch's `then` would resolve *new* observers, not the abort case. The real race: if `setQueryData` is called between the abort and the new fetch's `then`, the new fetch's data is set, but the abort-then-fail case observers (lines 128) are the OLD ones — they got rejected. OK so semantics are: if your call is aborted, you get an AbortError and must retry. The problem: the *caller* of `fetchQuery` doesn't know it was aborted vs. genuinely failed, so retry logic in pages will re-fetch and could double-load.
suggested_fix: In `fetchQuery` (line 81–86), if joining an in-flight call (`status === 'loading'`), propagate the same AbortController so the caller can detect cancellation. Or, in the catch at line 125, only reject-with-AbortError observers if the abort was caused by `opts.signal` from the caller (not by replacement), and otherwise resolve them with the new data.

title: `invalidate()` marks entries stale but never refetches — silent data divergence
description: Lines 181–186: `invalidate()` sets `entry.dataUpdatedAt = 0` but never triggers a refetch. The `refetch` option in opts (line 174) is declared but never read. So calling `queryClient.invalidate(['projects'])` does nothing observable. Components using `useQuery` with `staleTime > 0` will keep showing stale data forever. The docstring (line 175) claims 'Optionally refetch' but the implementation has no path for that.
suggested_fix: (a) Remove the unused `refetch` option from JSDoc. (b) Iterate `entry.effectDisposers` and call a per-entry refetch (re-run `fetchQuery(key, fetcher, { force: true })` for each subscriber). (c) Or expose a separate `queryClient.refetch(key)` and have callers chain it.

title: `useQuery` per-entry `_dataSignal` is set in `useQuery` but never updated by `fetchQuery` / `setQueryData` — useQuery hook gets stale data after mutations
description: Lines 232–234 create `entry._dataSignal` lazily inside `useQuery`. Lines 117 (`fetchQuery._executeEntry` then-branch) and 165 (`setQueryData`) update `entry.data` / `entry.status` but never touch `entry._dataSignal`. So if page A does `setQueryData(['projects'], [...])` and page B has a `useQuery(['projects'], ...)`, page B's effect (line 237) reads `dataSig.value` which is the INITIAL value (undefined) forever. The initial fetch in `useQuery` (line 247) *does* update `dataSig` (line 249), but only for the first fetch. Subsequent mutations are invisible to useQuery consumers.
suggested_fix: In `_executeEntry` success branch (line 117) and `setQueryData` (line 158), write to `entry._dataSignal?.value` if it exists. This is the same bug as React Query's `setQueryData` not notifying observers if the cache key was created via direct mutation.

title: `useQuery` `dispose` removes effect disposer but doesn't abort in-flight fetch or clear observers
description: Lines 260–267: dispose calls `dispose()` (the effect) and removes the effect from `entry.effectDisposers`. It does NOT call `entry.abortCtrl?.abort()`. So if a component unmounts mid-fetch, the request completes, populates `entry.data`, and triggers the *next* `useQuery` consumer. That's actually desired (cache is shared). But the entry's `observers` list is only cleared in `_executeEntry` then/catch (lines 123, 129, 135). If a useQuery consumer subscribes and never resolves (e.g. the page navigates away during the fetch), the observer's `resolve`/`reject` are held in memory until the fetch completes, holding a closure over the unmounted component.
suggested_fix: In `useQuery` dispose, if there are pending observers for this consumer specifically, remove them from `entry.observers`. (The cache layer doesn't track which consumer owns which observer; you need to wrap the consumer's promise so the consumer can reject it on dispose.)

title: `Promise.allSettled` + `unwrap` race: loading spinner removed on the *first* settled promise, not after both
description: Lines 41–46: `await Promise.allSettled([projects, activity])` then `container.removeChild(loading)` (line 46). `allSettled` waits for BOTH, so this is correct. BUT: the page has no error state — if `projects` rejected (line 56 checks `status !== 'fulfilled'`), the empty state is shown. If `activity` rejected, the `.catch(() => [])` on line 43 swallows it silently. So an activity fetch failure is invisible to the user AND to monitoring. There is no `console.warn` for the swallowed activity error.
suggested_fix: Surface activity failure via `console.warn('[dashboard] activity failed', err)`. Optionally show a degraded state (e.g. a small 'activity unavailable' note inside the activity card instead of the friendly empty state).

title: No `AbortController` — page navigation during slow fetch resolves against removed DOM, throws on `container.removeChild(loading)`
description: If the user clicks a sidebar link during the 2-second `/projects` fetch, `router.resolve()` clears `main` (app.js line 86 sets `main.innerHTML = ''`), then the new page mounts. The dashboard's still-running `await Promise.allSettled` eventually resolves. Line 46 calls `container.removeChild(loading)` — but `loading` was removed from the DOM when `main.innerHTML = ''` ran, so `container.removeChild(loading)` throws `NotFoundError: The node to be removed is not a child of this node`. Error swallowed by the `try/catch` in `app.js#loadPage`? No — `loadPage`'s try/catch is around the `importer()`, not around the page's body. The page's own unhandled rejection fires in console.
suggested_fix: (a) Pass an `AbortController` to `loadPage` and into the page handler. (b) In the page, check `signal.aborted` after every `await` and bail. (c) Wrap `container.removeChild(loading)` in `if (loading.parentNode === container)`. This is a recurring pattern — every page that does async work + DOM mutation needs the same guard.

title: `loadRecentProjects` no abort / no check — same race as dashboard, plus error path doesn't update sidebar on retry
description: Lines 98–135: async fetch, no AbortController. If the user navigates during the fetch, the same `container.innerHTML = ''` race occurs. More importantly, on error (line 127), the container is cleared and an error message shown — but there's no retry, no stale-cache fallback, no `queryClient` integration (despite `queryClient` being imported on line 23 and not used). So the sidebar projects are fetched via raw `apiGet` instead of the cache layer that the file's own module structure implies should be used.
suggested_fix: Use `queryClient.fetchQuery(['sidebar-projects'], ...)` so the data is shared with the dashboard's projects list. Add `signal` from `useQuery` and bail on `signal.aborted`. Add a retry button in the error state.

title: `doLogout` GET `/auth/signout` may not be a valid NextAuth endpoint; the fallback POST uses `setTimeout(300)` for navigation — flaky
description: Line 192: `await apiGet('/auth/signout', ...)` — NextAuth sign-out is POST, not GET. NextAuth may return 405 or 404 for GET. The `try/catch` swallows it. Line 196: fallback POST to `/api/auth/signout` with `csrfToken` in body. This might work but it's reinventing what `apiRequest` already does. After both, line 204 shows a success toast, then 300ms later hard-redirects. The 300ms is a magic number to let the toast render, but if the fetch is slow (it isn't awaited in the catch), the redirect happens BEFORE the response. No CSRF token validation: `readCookieCsrf()` (line 208) extracts only the first segment — NextAuth double-submit cookies are `token|hash` and the body should send the *hash* (per NextAuth docs), not the token. Possible CSRF bypass.
suggested_fix: Use `apiPost('/auth/signout', { csrfToken: readCookieCsrf() })` directly — don't GET. Send the HASH (split('|')[1]), not the token. Await the response. Replace the `setTimeout` with the promise chain: `await post; router.navigate('/login')`.

title: User menu button removeChild/appendChild race: `userBtn` is removed but the dropdown's button is not the userBtn — visible flicker
description: Lines 167–185: `userBtn` is created with `opButton(...)` and a `userBtn.setAttribute('aria-haspopup', 'menu')` (line 171). Then `userMenu = opDropdown(...)` creates its OWN button internally (line 229 in primitives). Line 184 does `header.removeChild(userBtn)`, line 185 does `header.appendChild(userMenu)`. Result: the placeholder button briefly renders, then is removed. Visual flicker, focus loss if the placeholder had focus, and the `aria-haspopup` attribute set on the *placeholder* is lost. The dropdown button doesn't have `aria-haspopup`.
suggested_fix: Don't create the placeholder button. Either: (a) have `opDropdown` accept a prop to set `aria-haspopup`, then set it after `userMenu` is created; or (b) refactor to `const userMenu = opDropdown(...); userMenu.setAttribute('aria-haspopup', 'menu'); header.appendChild(userMenu)` — single append, no remove.

title: `mountShell` registers no keyboard handler for sidebar collapse — keyboard users can't toggle
description: Line 147: `opButton` for sidebar collapse — mouse-only. No keyboard shortcut (e.g. `[` to toggle, common in OpenProject). The button is focusable (good) and clickable via Space/Enter (default button behavior), so a Tab user CAN click it. But there's no aria-pressed state reflecting the collapsed status, and no aria-controls pointing to the sidebar element.
suggested_fix: Add `aria-pressed` reflecting `sidebarCollapsed.value`, `aria-controls` to sidebar id. Add a global keydown for `Cmd/Ctrl+B` or `[` to toggle. Add `aria-expanded` instead/in addition.

title: No skip-link, no semantic landmarks — a11y fail for keyboard / screen reader users
description: `<div id="app">` with the splash inside, replaced by `<div class=

>` containing `<aside>` and `<header>` (good — shell.js uses landmarks). But there's no `<nav aria-label>` on the main nav (shell.js line 63 DOES set aria-label, good), and no `<main>` landmark at the HTML level — the `<main id="main

status"` and `aria-live="polite"` (good). However, when JS loads and the splash is removed, there's no focus management — focus stays on `<body>`, screen reader users don't get an announcement that the app is ready. No skip-to-content link.
suggested_fix:
Add `Skip to main content` at top of body. On `bootstrap` completion, move focus to `
` (or to the first h1) and announce via `aria-live`. Add `

` to main on first render.

title: `<noscript>` link to `/login` may 404 under SPA fallback if the dev server only serves index.html for non-/api paths
description: Line 33: `login page`. Under Vite's `appType: 'spa'`, navigation to `/login` (a non-asset, non-/api path) returns `index.html` (the SPA). So the no-JS user clicks login, gets the SPA, which is blank (since app.js didn't run). They can never log in. The comment in line 10–11 acknowledges Vite serving issues but doesn't address the noscript path.
suggested_fix: Either (a) build a static `login.html` for the no-JS fallback path, or (b) configure the Vite dev server to proxy `/login` to the Next.js server (where the real login page lives). For prod, the SPA fallback (nginx try_files) needs a carve-out for `/login`.

title: No `<meta name="description">` or theme-color — SEO/PWA basics missing
description: Line 18: `<title>OpenProject</title>` only. No description, no theme-color, no apple-touch-icon, no manifest link. PWA install won't work. Browser tab color in mobile is default.
suggested_fix: Add `<meta name="description" content="OpenProject — project management">`, `<meta name="theme-color" content="#1a67a3">`, `<link rel="manifest" href="/manifest.webmanifest">`, `<link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="/assets/apple-touch-icon.png">`.

title: Auth guard checks `currentUser.value` at route resolution time, not at session-change time — session expiry mid-session doesn't trigger redirect
description: Line 47–53: guard runs in `router.resolve()` for the current path. If session expires between navigations (e.g. JWT expires after 1h of inactivity), `currentUser.value` is still set (NextAuth client doesn't auto-refresh). The user keeps navigating, API calls return 401, each 401 triggers `location.href = '/login'` (api-client line 166) — which is correct. But the *guard* never fires for the current page; if the user clicks a link, they get the API 401 redirect. So the guard is defensive but not the primary auth boundary. The primary one (401 redirect) does a full reload, which is harsh (UI-6).
suggested_fix: On `onSessionChange` (app.js line 40), if a navigation is in progress and the session went null, navigate to /login. Add a `fetch` interceptor that, on any 401 from a non-initSession call, sets a `isAuthed = false` signal and lets the guard catch it.

title: `opEmpty` error message rendered via `e.message` — if error has no message, empty box; if message contains HTML, escaped by textContent (good)
description: Line 109–111: `message: e.message`. `opEmpty` uses `textContent` (primitives line 388) so HTML is escaped — good. But: (a) if `e.message` is empty/undefined, the user sees an empty error box with no info; (b) the message might be a low-level fetch error like 'Failed to fetch' which is useless. No error reporting to Sentry or any backend log.
suggested_fix: Provide a user-friendly fallback: `message: e.message || 'An unexpected error occurred. Please try again.'`. Add `console.error` for dev (already there) and consider `Sentry.captureException(e)` for prod. Show a 'Reload' button that calls `router.resolve()`.

title: `loadPage` doesn't call any cleanup from the previous page — listeners/effects on `main` persist
description: Lines 84–113: each call clears `main.innerHTML = ''` and re-mounts. Any event listeners added by the previous page (e.g. `window.addEventListener('keydown', ...)` for a key shortcut) stay on `window` and fire forever. Effects in `store.js` that the previous page created (e.g. via `effect(() => ...)` watching a signal) keep firing, holding references to the unmounted DOM. `queryClient.useQuery` consumers: their `dispose` is never called when the page unmounts (no contract for this). This is the classic SPA memory leak.
suggested_fix: Establish a page lifecycle: page module exports `{ mount, dispose }` or a function returning a dispose. `loadPage` calls the previous page's dispose before clearing. Or use `AbortController` per page and pass it down — every `addEventListener` and `effect` uses it for cleanup.

title: `router.currentPath()` and `router.currentQuery()` called inside `loadPage` after `await importer()` — race with concurrent nav
description: Lines 99–104: after the dynamic `import()` resolves (which can take 100s of ms on a slow connection), the code reads `router.currentPath()` and `router.currentQuery()`. If the user clicked a different link in the meantime, those return the NEW path, but the page being mounted was for the OLD path. The page receives mismatched params. This is a real race for slow networks.
suggested_fix: Capture path/query BEFORE the import: `const targetPath = router.currentPath(); const targetQuery = router.currentQuery(); const mod = await importer(); if (router.currentPath() !== targetPath) return /* stale */; mod.default({ ..., path: targetPath, query: targetQuery })`.

title: Routes registered as functions with no explicit `data-foo` patterns; `:projectId` matched as a single segment, not nested
description: Line 65–66: `'/projects/:projectId'` and `'/projects/:projectId/work-packages'`. The `matchPath` (router.js line 35) splits on `/` and checks length match. So `/projects/123` matches `:projectId = '123'`, fine. But `/projects/123/anything-else` does NOT match anything — the second pattern is 4 segments (`projects`, `:projectId`, `work-packages`), but `/projects/123/anything-else` is also 3 segments. So accessing `/projects/123/board` or `/projects/123/wiki` returns 404. The router has no nested-route / catchall / `*` pattern.
suggested_fix: Add wildcard support: `'/projects/:projectId/*'` -> params includes `splat: 'wiki'` or similar. Or document that the only valid project sub-routes are the ones explicitly registered, and add a 404 within the project context.

title: `_listeners` not exposed — `onRouteChange` returns a disposer (good) but no event is fired on guard-redirect (silent)
description: Line 102–109: `onRouteChange(fn)` adds a listener and returns a disposer — good. But `_notify(path)` (line 184) is only called after a route match or notFound, not after a guard redirect. If a guard returns a string and triggers `navigate(result, { replace: true })` (line 133), no `_notify` fires for the original path — the listener sees only the new path. If a component is `effect`-ing on `currentPath` to load data, it misses the intermediate state.
suggested_fix: Call `_notify(path)` before the guard redirect (with the rejected path), or document that listeners only fire on success. Add an `onBeforeRouteChange` hook separately.

title: Query string values `String(v)` — objects, arrays, booleans coerce to '[object Object]' or 'true'/'false'
description: Lines 117–121: `String(v)`. If a caller does `apiGet('/projects', { query: { filter: { active: true } } })`, this produces `?filter=%5Bobject%20Object%5D` — silently broken. Arrays become `1,2,3` (no key repetition). This is a footgun.
suggested_fix: Recursively serialize: objects → repeated keys, arrays → repeated keys, primitives → String. OR reject non-primitive query values at the type level and document. Use `URLSearchParams` with proper value coercion: `sp.append(k, String(v))` for primitives, `sp.append(k, v.map(String).join(','))` for arrays, throw for objects.

title: `readCookie` regex doesn't handle cookies set with `SameSite=Strict` and HttpOnly is moot but `decodeURIComponent` may throw on malformed cookies
description: Line 32: regex captures cookie value, then `decodeURIComponent(m[1])` (line 33). If the cookie value has a literal `%` followed by non-hex (e.g. a server bug puts `cookie=100%`), `decodeURIComponent` throws `URIError`. The function doesn't wrap in try/catch, so the error propagates up to `apiRequest` (line 143), which then proceeds *without* the CSRF header — request goes out, fails with 403, and the user sees a confusing error. No console.warn.
suggested_fix: Wrap `decodeURIComponent` in try/catch and return `null` on failure. Add `console.warn` for malformed cookies in dev.

title: `buildUrl` heuristic: any path starting with `/auth/` bypasses API prefix — fragile coupling to backend route names
description: Line 45: `if (path.startsWith('/auth/')) return path`. This works only if NextAuth's session/signin/signout/callback endpoints are mounted at `/auth/*` (not `/api/auth/*`). NextAuth default is `/api/auth/*`. If the project mounts NextAuth at `/auth/*` (per buildUrl's assumption), the API client's `apiGet('/auth/session')` (line 83) goes to `/auth/session` — but `initSession` is called with `SESSION_PATH = '/api/auth/session'` (line 23) directly, bypassing `buildUrl`. Inconsistent: `apiGet` users get `/auth/*`, `initSession` uses `/api/auth/*`. Two CSRF cookie names?
suggested_fix: Pick one: either all auth calls go through `apiGet` (so `buildUrl` decides), or all are explicit. Document the convention. Add an integration test that `initSession` and `apiGet('/auth/session')` hit the same URL.

title: `unwrap` throws `Error` subclass with `.status` and `.code` set as ad-hoc properties — no custom class for type-checking
description: Lines 217–222: `const err = new Error(...); err.status = ...; err.code = ...`. Callers can check `err.status === 401` but TypeScript users can't `instanceof ApiError` because there is no class. `console.error` in the catch path will print 'Error: HTTP 401' without the code, making debugging harder.
suggested_fix: Define `class ApiError extends Error { constructor(status, code, message, data) { super(message); this.name='ApiError'; this.status=status; this.code=code; this.data=data } }` and throw instances.

title: `a.innerHTML` template literals with escaped values — works but the static structure of the template is rebuilt per project
description: Lines 74–77, 99–102: each project list item does `a.innerHTML = template(...)` and the static HTML (the `<div>` wrappers with inline styles) is re-parsed for every item. For 100 projects, that's 100 HTML parses. Most code is static — only `${escapeHtml(...)}` is dynamic. Worse, inline styles in the template (`style="font-weight: var(--op-fw-medium)"`) are not cached by the browser; re-parsing the same string still incurs work.
suggested_fix: Build a DOM element once and clone it (or use a small template helper): create `<template>` with the static structure, then `cloneNode(true)` per item, set the dynamic text via `textContent`. Or use a `DocumentFragment` from a single template.

title: `escapeHtml` re-implemented locally — should be a shared util to avoid drift
description: Line 142: `function escapeHtml(s)` defined at the bottom of `dashboard.js`. If a new page (Phase 1+) needs to render user content via `innerHTML` and forgets to escape, the result is XSS. A shared util forces a single point of audit and lets you add tests. Currently the only other `escapeHtml` use is also in dashboard.js (lines 75, 76, 100) — but if Phase 1's work-package table or wiki page renders user content, it needs the same function.
suggested_fix: Move `escapeHtml` to `frontend/lib/escape.js` (or `frontend/utils/sanitize.js`). Export a `safeSetHTML(el, template, vars)` helper that does the escape-then-set in one call. Add unit tests for `<`, `>`, `&`, `"`, `'`, backtick, `javascript:` URL.

title: `opButton` `onClick` listener has no removal path — leaks if button is removed but listener stays
description: Line 35: `el.addEventListener('click', opts.onClick)`. If a button is created and removed without `removeEventListener`, the closure (which often captures page state) holds the page state alive. In tables with hundreds of action buttons, the leak is small per button but multiplies. The header docstring (line 8) acknowledges this and suggests `AbortController` — but the factories don't accept an `AbortSignal` to use.
suggested_fix: Add an optional `signal: AbortSignal` to every factory that adds listeners. Wire it: `opts.signal?.addEventListener('abort', () => el.removeEventListener('click', opts.onClick))`. Pages that fetch + render can pass the page's AbortController.signal.

title: `opSpinner` is a `<span>` not a `<div>` — inline element, may not respect block styling for centering
description: Line 351: `const wrap = document.createElement('span')`. A span is inline by default. `app.js` line 88 sets `sp.style.margin = 'var(--op-sp-12) auto'` and `sp.style.display = 'block'` (line 89), forcing block — but this is set by the caller, not the factory. If a caller forgets (or uses `opSpinner()` for inline loading next to a label), it sits inline and the margin auto doesn't center it. Also, an inline span with a `role` indicator (line 353 sets `aria-label`) is read by screen readers but the visual spinner is small.
suggested_fix: Use `<div>` by default with an `inline: true` opt to render as span. Document the expected display context. Or always render block and let callers wrap in inline contexts.

title: `import { toasts as toastsSignal, dismissToast } from '../../store.js'` and `import { effect } from '../../store.js'` — duplicate import statements
description: Lines 316–317: two import statements from the same module. Works fine (ES modules dedupe) but is style noise. Also a code smell: the file mixes primitives and toast-specific logic in one module. The toast system depends on the store; primitives shouldn't (separation of concerns).
suggested_fix: Move `opToastHost` (and its dep on `toasts`) into `frontend/components/feedback/toast.js` or `frontend/components/layout/toast-host.js`. Keep `primitives/index.js` for generic UI atoms with no store dependency.

title: `opDialog` body innerHTML path is the ONLY way to get string content — but JSDoc says `HTMLElement`
description: Same line as UI-7. JSDoc on line 160 says `body: HTMLElement`. Implementation accepts `string` and pipes to innerHTML. Mismatch invites misuse. Already covered in UI-7; this is the type-contract angle.
suggested_fix: Same as UI-7: make types match implementation OR remove string branch.

title: Entire page is a placeholder — no search input, no functionality, no tests
description: Lines 1–15: stub page that just renders an `opEmpty` with a 'coming in Phase 1' message. Test coverage: none. Accessibility: no `<h1>` semantic (uses `h.textContent = 'Search'` but it's an h1 — OK). No search input. The topbar (shell.js line 165 area) doesn't even have a search box yet, so users have no way to access this page except via URL bar.
suggested_fix: Add a real search input (debounced) wired to a backend endpoint. Add unit test for the page. Add a topbar search shortcut (Cmd+K) and link from the topbar to this page.

title: Placeholder page, no widget grid, no tests
description: Same shape as search.js. No functionality, no tests, no accessibility audit beyond the h1. The 'My page' menu entry in shell.js (line 176) navigates here — users clicking it see a useless empty state.
suggested_fix: Either (a) hide the topbar entry until Phase 1 ships, or (b) implement a minimal 'My assigned work packages' view pulling from the backend.

title: Help page tells user to use 'existing Next.js /help pages via the URL bar' — known broken UX
description: Lines 11–14: 'For now, use the existing Next.js /help pages via the URL bar.' But the entire frontend is the SPA — typing a URL routes through the SPA's history-based router (UI-28) and returns index.html, not the Next.js help page. So this advice doesn't work.
suggested_fix: Replace with a real help page (link to docs, FAQ, contact) OR remove the help nav entry until ready. If keeping the advice, change to 'use the /help route on the main OpenProject instance, not this beta'.

title: Projects index page is a placeholder — '+ New Project' button navigates to /projects/new which is not a registered route
description: Lines 9–13: '+ New Project' button calls `router.navigate('/projects/new')`. But app.js line 59 registers only `'/projects'` (no `:id/new`). router falls through to 404 handler (app.js line 69), which shows 'Page not found'. Clicking the button takes the user to a confusing 'Page not found' page.
suggested_fix: Either register `/projects/new` and ship the form, or remove the button until Phase 1. The dashboard's '+ New Project' button (line 28) has the same issue.

title: Project home placeholder, no actual project data fetched, no error state for missing project
description: Lines 1–15: page renders 'Project {params.projectId}' but never fetches the project. If `params.projectId` is invalid or the user has no access, the page shows the same placeholder. No 403/404 distinction. No way to know the project exists.
suggested_fix: Fetch project on mount via `apiGet('/projects/:id')`. If 404, show 'Project not found'. If 403, show 'No access'. Else show overview.

title: Work packages placeholder, same shape as project home — no data, no empty state, no tests
description: Lines 1–15: similar to UI-50. No fetch, no auth check. This is the page that Phase 3 will build (per comment).
suggested_fix: Same as UI-50. At minimum, fetch the project to confirm access.

title: Dead file — `_placeholder.js` is never imported, just a comment
description: Lines 1–2: comment-only file. No exports. No risk but cleanup debt.
suggested_fix: Delete the file OR convert to a README that documents the page handler pattern (it claims to, but the file is empty of content).

title: Test file exists but covers only 6 of 12 primitives — opDialog, opDropdown, opTabs, opToastHost, opTooltip, opCard have no tests
description: Lines 3–5: import list includes `opButton, opInput, opSelect, opCheckbox, opBadge, opSpinner, opEmpty` (7) but `opTextarea` is missing and the 5 interactive/feedback primitives are untested. No a11y tests, no XSS tests (the most critical risk — opDialog innerHTML).
suggested_fix: Add tests for: (a) opDialog accepts HTMLElement body and never accepts strings (security), (b) opDropdown's document click handler doesn't leak, (c) opTabs keyboard navigation, (d) opToastHost idempotency, (e) opTooltip viewport edge clamping, (f) XSS regression: pass a string with `<script>` to opDialog body and assert it's not executed.

title: Tests cover happy path; no test for: guard redirect, same-path-different-query nav, link interception, deep link popstate, race on guard redirect loop
description: Test file exists per `ls`. Without reading it, known untested: (a) `router.navigate` short-circuits same-path-different-query (UI-2 regression), (b) `popstate` listener accumulates under HMR (UI-4), (c) `extractParams` private API access (UI-1), (d) link interceptor's `dataset.external` opt-out (UI-3).
suggested_fix: Add regression tests for each finding in this report that affects router behavior. Use `jsdom` and trigger `popstate`, `click` on intercepted links, and a mock history.

title: Tests exist but should cover: malformed CSRF cookie, non-2xx error envelope unwrap, query string array/object coercion, 401 redirect loop guard
description: Same — test file exists per `ls`. Known gaps: (a) `readCookie` on `cookie=100%` throws (UI-37), (b) `unwrap` on `{ error: { code, message } }` envelope (UI-39), (c) `apiGet(..., { query: { filter: { x: 1 } } })` produces broken URL (UI-36), (d) 401 redirect doesn't loop (UI-6), (e) `buildUrl` consistency between `initSession` and `apiGet('/auth/...')` (UI-38).
suggested_fix: Add tests for each. Mock `document.cookie`, `fetch`, `location.href`.

title: Tests exist but `useQuery` second consumer after `setQueryData` should observe new data — bug UI-19 not covered
description: Per `ls`, file exists at line 35 shows a `fetcher` mock. Likely tests cache hit/miss, dedup, abort — but not the `_dataSignal` bug. Without reading, expected gap: no test for `useQuery` consumer A updates `setQueryData`, consumer B (same key) sees new data.
suggested_fix: Add: `it('useQuery B observes setQueryData from useQuery A', ...)` and `it('invalidate marks stale without refetching', ...)` (UI-18).

title: Tests cover subscribe/dispose/effect — no test for: batch semantics, effect re-subscribe dedupe, dispose-during-effect
description: Per `ls`, lines 23–67 cover subscribe + dispose + effect. Likely gaps: (a) `batch(() => { sig1.value = 1; sig2.value = 2 })` notifies once (UI-16), (b) effect that subscribes to two signals and unsubscribes from one — proper cleanup (UI-15).
suggested_fix: Add batch test and effect-cleanup-mid-run test.

title: E2E test exists but no coverage for: deep linking to a project URL, 401 redirect, browser back/forward preserves state, mobile viewport (375x667)
description: Per `ls`, e2e/phase0.spec.js exists. The 'no coverage' items: (a) typing `/projects/123` directly in URL bar loads the project page (deep link / refresh — relies on Vite serving /index.html), (b) session expiry mid-nav redirects to /login, (c) browser back after clicking a link returns to the previous page's state, (d) mobile viewport rendering: sidebar collapse behavior, topbar overflow.
suggested_fix: Add Playwright (or whatever) tests: deep-link with refresh, 401 simulation (mock), back-button state preservation, mobile viewport snapshot.

title: Styles imported as side-effect modules — Vite handles, but no `@media` query for mobile / sidebar overlay
description: Per grep, only 2 `@media` matches across all CSS (layout.css and tokens.css). Need to verify these cover: (a) mobile breakpoint that hides sidebar behind a toggle vs. inline, (b) topbar collapses to icon-only on narrow viewports, (c) dialog becomes full-width on mobile. Without reading the CSS, expected gap: sidebar behavior on <768px viewport.
suggested_fix: Add `@media (max-width: 768px) { .op-sidebar { position: fixed; transform: translateX(-100%); transition: transform 0.2s; } .op-shell--collapsed .op-sidebar { transform: translateX(0); } .op-topbar { padding: 0.5rem; } .op-dialog { max-width: 100%; } }`. Test with Playwright mobile viewport.

title: `dispose` returned from `mountShell` is never called — shell is mounted once at bootstrap, so OK, but the API surface implies callers should dispose
description: Lines 31, 55: `mountShell` returns `dispose`. No one in `app.js` or anywhere else calls it. Since the shell is a singleton (mounted once), this is fine. But the API surface (return value) implies it's part of the lifecycle, and if a future `unmountShell` is added (e.g. for a full app reset), the contract isn't clear. The `effect` for `sidebarCollapsed` (line 51) is the only disposer — if the shell IS ever torn down, you want to also abort `loadRecentProjects` (line 92), which currently has no abort.
suggested_fix: Either: (a) return no `dispose` (make it explicit that the shell is permanent), or (b) wire `loadRecentProjects` to use an AbortController and abort it in `dispose`. Add a comment that the shell is permanent for now.

title: `queryClient` imported from `'../../query.js'` but never used in this file
description: Line 23: `import { queryClient } from '../../query.js'`. The import is unused (search confirms: only declaration, no usage in this file). Dead code / dead import.
suggested_fix: Remove the unused import. If the plan is to use it for `loadRecentProjects`, do so (UI-23).

title: `theme` and `sidebarCollapsed` read `localStorage` at module load — fails in SSR (Vite SSR) and in environments without localStorage
description: Lines 178, 182: `signal(localStorage.getItem('op-theme') || 'light')`. If this module is ever imported in a Node.js context (SSR, tests, build-time tools), `localStorage` is undefined → ReferenceError. Tests probably stub it (test/setup.js likely has a mock). For Vite SSR, this breaks.
suggested_fix: Guard: `typeof localStorage !== 'undefined' ? localStorage.getItem('op-theme') : 'light'`. Or use a `try { localStorage.getItem(...) } catch { 'light' }`.

title: `theme.subscribe(t => localStorage.setItem('op-theme', t))` runs at module load — first run may not be the user's choice
description: Line 179: subscribe is called immediately, which fires once with current value (synchronous? Let's check Signal.subscribe at line 67 — it just `add`s to subs, no immediate fire). So the subscribe is correct. BUT: the initial value of `theme` is `localStorage.getItem('op-theme') || 'light'`. If the user has no preference and 'light' is returned, the subscribe never fires (no value change). The setItem never runs. That's fine. The risk: if a test or another module mutates the signal early, the write happens, but the test cleanup doesn't reset localStorage → test isolation broken.
suggested_fix: Document this side effect. In tests, mock `localStorage` (test/setup.js may already).

title: `cache` is a module-level Map — never garbage collected; grows unbounded for keys with high cardinality
description: Line 38: `const cache = new Map()`. Pages that fetch per-id (`['work-package', id]`) for many ids accumulate entries. No LRU, no TTL eviction. For a long-running session browsing hundreds of work packages, the cache grows. `removeQuery` exists (line 193) but is never called by consumers.
suggested_fix: Add `staleTime + gcTime` semantics: entries older than `gcTime` (default 5 min) without observers are removed. Or implement a simple LRU with a max size (e.g. 100 entries). Add a `queryClient.gc()` method and call on route change.

title: `_executeEntry` is a method but documented as internal — no `_` prefix; should be private or moved out
description: Line 104: `_executeEntry` is the convention for "private" (single underscore), but it's a public method on the class. Consumers could call it directly. Not a bug but loose encapsulation.
suggested_fix: Rename to `#executeEntry` (true private via class field) and document the entry shape as @internal in JSDoc. Or move to a module-level function.

title: `useQuery` initial state copies `getQueryData(key)` and `getQueryStatus(key)` but ignores the entry's `error` field — stale errors invisible
description: Lines 219–223: `state = signal({ data: ..., status: ..., error: null })`. `getQueryData` returns entry.data, but the error path is hardcoded `null`. If the entry exists in cache with `status: 'error'` and an `error` (from a prior failed fetch), the initial state hides the error. Consumers calling `useQuery(...).error()` get null when there was a real error. The new fetch (line 247) will retry, but until it resolves, the error is invisible.
suggested_fix: Add `getQueryError(key)` and include it in the initial state. Or fold it into `getQueryStatus` to return `{data, status, error}` as a tuple.

title: `matchPath` uses `decodeURIComponent` on the segment — throws on malformed URI
description: Line 42: `params[pp[i].slice(1)] = decodeURIComponent(ap[i])`. If the URL has a malformed escape (e.g. `/projects/abc%`) the function throws `URIError`. The error propagates to `router.resolve()` (line 138) — wait, the for loop is inside `try`? No, line 138 is `for (const r of this.routes) { const params = matchPath(...); ... }` — no try around the match. So a malformed URL crashes `resolve()`. The guard runs first (line 130) — if it returns a redirect, the user is sent to /login. Otherwise, matchPath throws and the whole router is broken until reload.
suggested_fix: Wrap `decodeURIComponent` in try/catch and treat malformed escapes as no-match (return null). The pattern won't match and the next route is tried; if all fail, 404 handler shows.

title: `parseQuery` doesn't decode values (URLSearchParams does this internally, so this is fine) — but doesn't handle multiple values per key
description: Line 51–58: `for (const [k, v] of sp) { if (!(k in out)) out[k] = v }`. First value wins. If a backend expects `?tag=a&tag=b`, the client receives `{tag: 'a'}`. `URLSearchParams.getAll(k)` would give both. The first-wins behavior is documented in line 50 — fine. But silent truncation is a footgun.
suggested_fix: Document that query params are first-wins. If multi-value needed, expose `parseQueryAll(qs)` returning `Record<string, string[]>`. Add a test.

title: `currentQuery()` reads `location.search` on every call — not memoized; can drift from `_currentPath` updates
description: Line 116: `parseQuery(location.search.slice(1))`. No memoization. If the page reads `router.currentQuery()` once and then later, and the URL changes between (via `popstate`), the second call gets the new query but `_currentPath` was updated by `resolve()` — they could be temporarily out of sync if read in between. Minor.
suggested_fix: Store the latest query on `resolve()` and return cached value. Or accept the eventual consistency.

title: Route `/` registered as a redirect to `/dashboard` — but it does `router.navigate('/dashboard')` inside the handler, which triggers a full re-resolve, two history entries
description: Line 57: `router.add('/', () => navigateTo('/dashboard'))`. The handler calls `router.navigate('/dashboard')` which does `pushState` + `resolve()`. Result: history has BOTH `/` and `/dashboard` entries. User clicks back from /dashboard → returns to `/` → handler fires again → navigates to /dashboard → infinite loop on back-button. (Actually: if the path is unchanged, the navigate short-circuits, but the URL bar shows `/`, the handler fires, navigates to /dashboard, the back-button then goes to /dashboard's previous entry, not `/`. So no infinite loop, but the back-button is broken: user can never go back past the initial load.)
suggested_fix: Use `router.replace('/dashboard')` (which uses `history.replaceState`) for the root → /dashboard redirect, so the URL bar updates without a history entry. Or use a server-side redirect.

title: No global error boundary — an unhandled error in a page handler crashes the app to a blank screen
description: `bootstrap()` has no `window.addEventListener('error', ...)` or `unhandledrejection` handler. If a page handler throws (e.g. `params.projectId` is undefined and code does `params.projectId.toUpperCase()`), the error propagates to `loadPage`'s try/catch (line 105), which renders the error UI. Good. But errors in async code OUTSIDE `loadPage` (e.g. a `setTimeout` callback in a page) are unhandled. The whole `<div id="app">` could be left in a half-mounted state.
suggested_fix: Add `window.addEventListener('error', ...)` and `'unhandledrejection'` at bootstrap, render a friendly 'Something went wrong' UI into `#app` if it happens. Use `Sentry.captureException` for prod.

title: `opInput` `required` asterisk is in the label text (`+ ' *'`) — screen reader reads the asterisk literally, not 'required'
description: Line 49: `lbl.textContent = opts.label + (opts.required ? ' *' : '')`. The `*` is rendered as a visible marker but not announced as 'required' by screen readers (it's read as 'asterisk' or just included in the label). WAI-ARIA recommendation: add `aria-required="true"` to the input AND make the asterisk `aria-hidden="true"` (or use CSS `::after`).
suggested_fix: Add `el.setAttribute('aria-required', 'true')` for required inputs. Wrap the `*` in `*`. Add ` (required)` for screen readers.

title: `opCheckbox` label is inside the wrap (`<label>`) but not associated via `for` — only works if input is nested
description: Line 140–154: `<label>` wraps the input. The label is `span.textContent = opts.label` appended. The implicit label association (input nested inside label) works, but `lbl.htmlFor` isn't set. If a future refactor moves the input outside the label, the association breaks silently. WAI-ARIA best practice: explicit `for`/`id`.
suggested_fix: Add an id to the input (`el.id = 'op-cb-' + (opts.name || Math.random())`) and `lbl.htmlFor = el.id`. Same for opInput.

title: `opTextarea` has no `aria-invalid` / `error` styling like `opInput` has — inconsistent
description: Line 86–105: no `error` prop, no `aria-invalid`, no `op-help` / `op-error` text. The `opInput` factory has all of these (lines 71–76). For a content-creation app like OpenProject, textareas are used heavily (work-package description, wiki page body). No validation feedback at all.
suggested_fix: Mirror opInput: add `error`, `help`, `required` props. Set `aria-invalid`, `aria-describedby` pointing to help/error ids.

title: `opSelect` no `aria-invalid`, no help/error text, no `required` indicator — same gap as opTextarea
description: Line 111–133: same issue as opTextarea. For project status / type / assignee pickers, this matters.
suggested_fix: Mirror opInput: add `error`, `help`, `required` props with aria attributes.

title: Sidebar nav uses `textContent = l.icon + '  ' + l.label` — icon and label are not announced separately, no `aria-hidden` on icon
description: Line 74: `a.textContent = l.icon + '  ' + l.label`. The icon is a Unicode glyph (`◫`, `⊞`) that screen readers will read aloud ('white square with horizontal lines', 'boxed plus'). Visual decoration that pollutes the SR experience.
suggested_fix: Wrap the icon in `${l.icon}` and set `a.textContent = l.label` (or append label as a separate text node). Use the existing textContent but add `aria-label` to override.

title: Sidebar links are not marked as the current page (no `aria-current="page"`) — a11y miss
description: Lines 70–76: links created with no `aria-current`. The user has no way to know which page they're on from a screen reader perspective, beyond the page's `<h1>`. WAI-ARIA recommends `aria-current="page"` on the current nav link.
suggested_fix: Use an `effect` watching `currentPath` to update `aria-current` on the matching link. Or in shell.js, set it on mount based on initial path, and re-run on route change.

title: `rootEl.innerHTML = ''` followed by `appendChild(container)` — same innerHTML pattern; safer to use `replaceChildren(container)`
description: Line 47–48: `rootEl.innerHTML = ''` then `rootEl.appendChild(container)`. This is a common pattern but `innerHTML = ''` is slower and has security concerns if the container's contents include user data. `replaceChildren(container)` is the modern API.
suggested_fix: Use `rootEl.replaceChildren(container)` (a single DOM call, no innerHTML). Same for the other `innerHTML = ''` patterns in app.js (lines 70, 86, 103, 107), shell.js (lines 99, 105, 127), primitives (lines 194, 305, 333), and dashboard.js (lines 74, 99).

title: `pushToast` uses `Math.random()` for id — collision possible in rapid bursts
description: Line 195: `'t' + Math.random().toString(36).slice(2, 10)` — 8 base36 chars = ~36^8 = 2.8 trillion combinations. Collision probability for 1000 toasts in a session is negligible, but if two toasts are pushed in the same microtask (synchronous code), the random values are based on the same `Math.random` state and may collide. The `dismissToast(id)` filter at line 203 would remove the wrong toast.
suggested_fix: Use a monotonic counter: `let _id = 0; const id = 't' + (++_id)`. Or use `crypto.randomUUID()`.

title: `opBadge` is a `<span>` — should be inside semantic context; no role or aria
description: Line 366: `<span class='op-badge'>`. Spans are generic inline elements. If a badge conveys state (e.g. 'overdue', 'blocked'), screen reader users miss it. WAI-ARIA suggests `role="status"` for state indicators.
suggested_fix: Accept an optional `label` or `srText` prop. Render `<span role=
aria-label={srText || text}>{text}`. Or document that badges must be adjacent to a text label that conveys the same info.

title: Non-204 + non-JSON response: tries `res.text()` — but a binary response (e.g. file download) will corrupt as UTF-8 string
description: Line 179–184: `data = await res.text()`. If the response is an image or PDF (e.g. a work-package attachment download), the binary bytes are decoded as UTF-8, producing a corrupted string. The caller has no way to know it was binary.
suggested_fix: Return a `dataType: 'json' | 'text' | 'binary'` flag, or use `res.blob()` and let the caller decide. For now, check `contentType` header before reading the body.

title: `opSpinner` is purely CSS animation — no test, no reduced-motion respect (a11y)
description: Line 350–359: span with class `op-spinner`. CSS animation in tokens.css / layout.css. No `prefers-reduced-motion` media query. Users with vestibular disorders see a constant spinning animation. WCAG 2.3.3 (Animation from Interactions) recommends respecting this.
suggested_fix: In the CSS, add: `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { .op-spinner { animation: none; } }`. Or render a static 'Loading…' text alternative for reduced-motion users.

title: `_notify` doesn't catch listener errors — one bad listener breaks all subsequent notifications
description: Line 184–186: `for (const fn of this._listeners) fn(path)`. If `fn` throws, the loop stops, no further listeners fire. A bug in one subscriber silently breaks others.
suggested_fix: Wrap each call in try/catch: `try { fn(path) } catch (e) { console.error('[router] listener threw', e) }`. Same pattern for `for (const sub of [...this.#subs]) sub()` in store.js (line 57).

title: `computed(fn)` returns `{ value, peek }` — not a Signal — breaks the duck-type assumption of other code
description: Line 144–153: `computed` returns a plain object, not a `Signal`. Code that does `if (x instanceof Signal)` or `x.subscribe(...)` will fail. Code that does `x.value = y` (which is a setter on Signal) will fail silently. Inconsistent API surface.
suggested_fix: Make `computed` return a `Signal` (with read-only `.value` and a guarded setter that throws) or document the difference. The `peek()` method is redundant — `value` (read) is the same.

title: `newEntry()` factory: `dataUpdatedAt: 0` is fresh-from-epoch → always stale on first check
description: Line 53: `dataUpdatedAt: 0`. In `fetchQuery` (line 78–79), `fresh = entry.status === 'success' && (Date.now() - entry.dataUpdatedAt) < staleTime`. With `dataUpdatedAt: 0`, the check is `Date.now() < staleTime` which is true for any `staleTime > 0`. Wait, that means it's FRESH (staleTime: 0 = always stale, staleTime: 1000 = fresh for 1s). Hmm. Re-read: `fresh` means the cache is FRESH (not stale). The condition for FRESH is `dataUpdatedAt recent`. With `dataUpdatedAt: 0` (initial), it's NOT recent, so `fresh = false`, so it refetches. Correct. But the semantic of `dataUpdatedAt: 0` is 'never updated' — which is fine here, just confusing. The bug is elsewhere: `entry.status = 'idle'` (line 51) doesn't enter the loading-dedup branch (line 81) — wait, `entry.status === 'loading'` is the dedup check. On first call, status is 'idle', so dedup doesn't kick in. Correct. On second call WHILE first is in flight, status is 'loading', dedup kicks in. Correct.
suggested_fix: Rename `dataUpdatedAt` to `lastFetchedAt` or `cachedAt` for clarity. Add a JSDoc comment explaining the freshness check.

title: `keyToString` uses `JSON.stringify(key)` — keys with `undefined` values stringify as `null`, collisions possible
description: Line 45: `JSON.stringify(['projects', undefined, 'work-packages'])` = `'["projects",null,"work-packages"]'`. So `['projects', null, 'work-packages']` and `['projects', undefined, 'work-packages']` collide. Same for functions (skipped) and symbols (skipped → undefined).
suggested_fix: Use a separator-based key encoding: `key.map(v => String(v ?? '__null__')).join('|')`. Or canonicalize the key (replace undefined with a sentinel) before stringifying.

title: No CSRF / XSS guidance in component docs — factory accepts `string` for body/panel but no warning that callers must sanitize
description: Header comment (lines 1–16) explains the conventions but doesn't mention security. The `innerHTML` patterns in `opDialog` and `opTabs` are XSS sinks, but a developer adding a new component might not know.
suggested_fix: Add a 'Security' section: 'NEVER pass user-controlled strings to opDialog.body or opTabs.panel — only pass HTMLElement. Use textContent for user data. See frontend/lib/escape.js for the safe helper.'

title: 'Quick Links' hardcoded list — `/notifications` not registered, will 404
description: Lines 112–117: link list includes `/notifications` (line 115). The router has no `/notifications` route (app.js lines 57–66). Clicking it hits the 404 handler. Same for `/settings/security` in shell.js (line 177).
suggested_fix: Either implement the routes or remove the links until they exist. Add a `// TODO: Phase X` marker per link.

title: `opTabs` panel id missing for `aria-controls` linkage (UI-12) — also tab button id missing for `aria-labelledby`
description: Same as UI-12 but the type-system angle. Without ids, the keyboard nav upgrade (UI-12) and `aria-controls` / `aria-labelledby` are not implementable cleanly.
suggested_fix: Generate ids per tab + panel in `opTabs` factory: `id-op-tabs-${counter++}-${t.id}`. Add `aria-controls` to tab, `aria-labelledby` to panel.

title: `/^https?:///.test(href)` early-returns BEFORE checking `a.origin` — works for `http://` URLs but not for `data:`, `blob:`, `chrome-extension:`, etc.
description: Line 213–215: external check is `https?://`, `mailto:`, `tel:`, and `a.origin !== location.origin`. But `data:` URIs (e.g. `<a href="data:text/html,...">`) pass none of these — the interceptor tries to `navigate(href)` which calls `pushState` with the data URI. Browser then navigates to the data URI on the next interaction. This is a niche XSS vector if a page can be tricked into rendering a data URI link.
suggested_fix: Reject more schemes: `if (/^(https?:|data:|javascript:|blob:|file:|chrome-extension:)/i.test(href)) return`. Or check `a.protocol` against an allowlist.
